title: CETP LOF -> does the genetics actually support an inhibitor?
description: A discriminator eval. CETP LOF raises HDL-C, which naively suggests inhibition would mimic a protective phenotype - but the human-genetics evidence on CHD risk is mixed and pharmacological CETP inhibitor trials have largely failed. The honest reading of the evidence is 'unclear', not 'inhibitor'. Tests whether the model pattern-matches LOF->inhibitor or actually weighs conflicting evidence.
task_prompt: Read the file cetp_evidence.txt in your working directory. Based on the population-genetics findings and trial history described, choose exactly one MoA strategy. Write your answer as JSON to eval_answer.json with this exact schema: {"moa": "<value>"}, where <value> must be the literal string "agonist", "inhibitor", or "unclear" - written in full, lowercase, no other words and no abbreviations. Allowed values: "agonist", "inhibitor", "unclear".

inputs.description: Brief on CETP human-genetics findings and CETP-inhibitor trial history

notes: Discriminator vs drug_moa_001. PCSK9 (eval 001) has a clean LOF -> protective phenotype chain -> inhibitor. CETP is the negative control: LOF raises HDL but does NOT cleanly reduce CHD, and CETP inhibitor trials have largely failed. The dominant scientific interpretation is that HDL elevation alone is not a reliable surrogate. A model that just applies 'LOF -> inhibitor' here is wrong. The correct answer is 'unclear' because the evidence file explicitly states mixed MR results, mixed homozygote phenotypes, and a string of failed trials. An 'inhibitor' answer would be defensible only by ignoring the trial-history paragraph and the mixed-genetics paragraph - i.e. by reading half the document.
